sections.heading: management of personal information
sections.title: Open and transparent management of personal information
sections.summary: APP entities must implement practices to comply with Australian Privacy Principles and registered codes. They must also handle individual inquiries about compliance with these principles. The entity must maintain an up-to-date privacy policy that details collected information, collection methods, purposes, access rights, complaint procedures, overseas disclosure, and recipient countries.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires clear documentation of data handling practices and individual access rights, critical for assessing transparency in cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: APP entities must take reasonable steps to implement practices ensuring compliance with Australian Privacy Principles and registered APP codes.
APP entities must take reasonable steps to handle individual inquiries about compliance with Australian Privacy Principles or registered APP codes.
APP entities must have a clearly expressed and up-to-date policy on personal information management.
APP privacy policy must detail collected personal information types.
APP privacy policy must specify how personal information is collected and held.
APP privacy policy must state purposes for collecting, holding, using, and disclosing personal information.
APP privacy policy must outline individual access to and correction of personal information.
APP privacy policy must describe complaint procedures for privacy breaches.
APP privacy policy must indicate if overseas disclosure of personal information is likely.
APP privacy policy must list countries where overseas recipients may be located if practicable.
APP entities must make their privacy policy available in reasonable steps.
APP entities must provide copies of privacy policy in requested forms.

sections.source_quote:
management of personal information
1.1 The object of this principle is to ensure that APP entities manage personal information in an open and transparent way. Compliance with the Australian Privacy Principles etc.

sections.heading: 2  Australian Privacy Principle 2—anonymity and pseudonymity
sections.title: Option for anonymity or pseudonymity in transactions
sections.summary: Individuals must have the option to remain anonymous or use a pseudonym when interacting with APP entities. This does not apply if the entity is legally required to deal with identified individuals or if it is impracticable to handle anonymous individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows individuals to maintain anonymity in data interactions, relevant for assessing privacy in cross-border data transfers where anonymity is a concern.

requirements.text: Individuals must have the option of not identifying themselves or using a pseudonym when dealing with APP entities.
This does not apply if the APP entity is legally required or authorized by Australian law to deal with identified individuals.
This does not apply if it is impracticable for the entity to deal with anonymous individuals.

sections.source_quote: 2.1 Individuals must have the option of not identifying themselves, or of using a pseudonym, when dealing with an APP entity in relation to a particular matter.

sections.heading: Personal information other than sensitive information
sections.title: Collection of personal information with necessary purpose
sections.summary: APP entities must not collect personal information (other than sensitive information) unless it is reasonably necessary for their functions. Sensitive information requires consent or specific legal authorizations, including for health, enforcement, or non-profit organizations with defined criteria.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets strict conditions for data collection, critical for assessing whether data transfers meet necessary purpose requirements.

requirements.text: APP entities must not collect personal information (other than sensitive information) unless reasonably necessary for functions.
APP entities must not collect sensitive information without individual consent or specific legal authorizations.
Consent for sensitive information must be reasonably necessary for functions.
Collection of sensitive information may be authorized by Australian law or court order.
Permitted general situations may allow collection of sensitive information.
Permitted health situations may allow collection of sensitive information for health organizations.
Enforcement bodies may collect sensitive information if reasonably necessary for enforcement activities.
Non-profit organizations may collect sensitive information if it relates to their activities and members.

sections.source_quote: 3.1 If an APP entity is an agency, the entity must not collect personal information (other than sensitive information) unless the information is reasonably necessary for, or directly related to, one or more of the entity's functions or activities.

sections.heading: information
sections.title: Handling unsolicited personal information
sections.summary: APP entities must determine if unsolicited personal information could have been collected under Australian Privacy Principle 3. If not, they must destroy the information or ensure it is de-identified. Otherwise, Australian Privacy Principles 5-13 apply.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires assessment of unsolicited data to prevent unnecessary data retention, critical for DTIA in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: APP entities must determine if unsolicited personal information could have been collected under Australian Privacy Principle 3.
APP entities may use unsolicited information for determining compliance with Australian Privacy Principle 3.
If unsolicited information could not have been collected, entities must destroy or de-identify it.

sections.source_quote: 4.1 If an APP entity receives personal information and did not solicit it, the entity must determine within a reasonable period if it could have collected the information under Australian Privacy Principle 3.

sections.heading: 5  Australian Privacy Principle 5—notification of the collection of
sections.title: Notification of personal information collection
sections.summary: APP entities must notify individuals of specific matters related to personal information collection, including entity details, collection circumstances, legal basis, purposes, consequences of non-collection, disclosure recipients, and privacy policy access procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates detailed notification for data transfers, essential for assessing transparency and compliance in cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: APP entities must notify individuals of entity identity, contact details, and collection circumstances.
APP entities must notify individuals if information is collected from others or they are unaware of collection.
APP entities must notify individuals if collection is legally required or authorized.
APP entities must state purposes for collecting personal information.
APP entities must inform individuals of consequences if information is not collected.
APP entities must disclose other entities that receive personal information.
APP entities must indicate if privacy policy includes access and correction procedures.
APP entities must indicate if privacy policy includes complaint procedures.
APP entities must state if overseas disclosure is likely.
APP entities must list countries where overseas recipients may be located.

sections.source_quote: 5.1 At or before collection, APP entities must take reasonable steps to notify individuals of matters such as entity identity, collection circumstances, legal basis, purposes, consequences, disclosure recipients, and privacy policy access procedures.

sections.heading: information
sections.title: Use or disclosure of personal information with purpose limitation
sections.summary: APP entities must not use or disclose personal information for secondary purposes without individual consent, specific legal authorizations, or permitted situations. Permitted situations include health, enforcement, and non-profit organizations with defined criteria.
sections.dtia_summary: Enforces purpose limitation for data transfers, critical for assessing whether data is used appropriately across borders.

requirements.text: APP entities must not use or disclose personal information for secondary purposes without individual consent.
APP entities must not use or disclose personal information for secondary purposes without specific legal authorizations.
Permitted general situations may allow use or disclosure of information.
Permitted health situations may allow use or disclosure of information for health organizations.
Enforcement bodies may use information if reasonably necessary for enforcement activities.
Biometric information may be disclosed to enforcement bodies with specific guidelines.

sections.source_quote: 6.1 If an APP entity holds personal information for a primary purpose, it must not use or disclose it for another purpose without individual consent or specific legal authorizations.

sections.heading: Direct marketing
sections.title: Direct marketing consent and opt-out requirements
sections.summary: Organisations must not use or disclose personal information for direct marketing unless specific conditions are met. Exceptions include obtaining consent, the individual reasonably expects marketing, providing an easy opt-out mechanism, and the individual not having opted out. Sensitive information requires explicit consent for direct marketing. Organisations must provide a simple opt-out method and respond to opt-out requests within a reasonable period without charging fees.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit consent or opt-out mechanisms for direct marketing across borders; mandates clear opt-out processes to ensure data subjects can control their marketing communications.

requirements.text: Organisation must not use or disclose personal information for direct marketing
Organisation may use personal information for direct marketing if individual reasonably expects use
Organisation must provide simple opt-out mechanism for direct marketing
Individual must not have opted out for direct marketing
Organisation must respond to opt-out requests within reasonable period
Organisation must not charge individual for opt-out requests

sections.source_quote: Direct marketing: If an organisation holds personal information about an individual, the organisation must not use or disclose the information for the purpose of direct marketing.

sections.heading: 8  Australian Privacy Principle 8—cross-border disclosure of
sections.title: Cross-border data disclosure safeguards
sections.summary: Before disclosing personal information to overseas recipients, entities must ensure the recipient does not breach Australian Privacy Principles. Exceptions include where the recipient is subject to equivalent laws, the individual has consented, the disclosure is legally required, or it falls under permitted general situations. Entities must verify that the recipient's protections are substantially similar to Australian standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for cross-border data transfers, requiring recipient compliance with Australian privacy standards or equivalent protections to ensure data transfers are secure and lawful.

requirements.text: Entity must ensure overseas recipient does not breach Australian Privacy Principles
Recipient must have equivalent legal protections to Australian Privacy Principles
Entity must provide individual consent for cross-border disclosure
Disclosure may be legally required or authorized by Australian law
Entity must verify recipient is subject to prescribed country or binding scheme

sections.source_quote: Before an APP entity discloses personal information about an individual to a person (the overseas recipient) who is not in Australia or an external Territory and who is not the entity or the individual, the entity must ensure the overseas recipient does not breach the Australian Privacy Principles.

sections.heading: Adoption of government related identifiers
sections.title: Government identifiers usage restrictions
sections.summary: Organisations must not adopt government-related identifiers without legal authorization or regulatory approval. Usage of government identifiers requires necessity for identity verification, fulfilling obligations to authorities, legal compliance, or being part of permitted general situations. Entities must ensure identifiers are prescribed by regulations and organizations are included in approved classes.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border data transfers involving government identifiers by requiring regulatory approval and necessity for identity verification or legal compliance.

requirements.text: Organisation must not adopt government-related identifiers without legal authorization
Use of government identifiers must be necessary for identity verification
Use of government identifiers must be necessary for fulfilling obligations to authorities
Use of government identifiers must be legally authorized

sections.source_quote: An organisation must not adopt a government related identifier of an individual as its own identifier of the individual unless the adoption is required or authorised by or under an Australian law.

sections.heading: 10  Australian Privacy Principle 10—quality of personal information
sections.title: Personal information accuracy and completeness
sections.summary: Entities must ensure personal information is accurate, up-to-date, and complete when collected and when used or disclosed. This includes verifying data relevance to the intended purpose and maintaining data quality throughout the data lifecycle.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures data quality for cross-border transfers by requiring accurate, up-to-date, and complete personal information to prevent data misuse and ensure compliance with privacy standards.

requirements.text: Entity must ensure personal information is accurate, up-to-date, and complete
Entity must ensure personal information is relevant to the purpose of use or disclosure

sections.source_quote: An APP entity must take such steps (if any) as are reasonable in the circumstances to ensure that the personal information that the entity collects is accurate, up-to-date and complete.

sections.heading: information
sections.title: Personal information security measures
sections.summary: Entities must implement technical and organizational measures to protect personal information from unauthorized access, use, or disclosure. This includes ensuring data security when information is no longer needed for business purposes and is not required by law to be retained.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires security measures for cross-border data transfers to protect personal information from breaches and unauthorized access, ensuring data remains secure during transfer and storage.

requirements.text: Entity must take reasonable steps to protect personal information
Entity must ensure information is destroyed or securely handled when no longer needed

sections.source_quote: If an APP entity holds personal information, the entity must take such steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to protect the information.

sections.heading: Access
sections.title: Individual access to personal information
sections.summary: Entities must provide individuals with access to their personal information upon request, with exceptions for legal obligations, privacy risks, or commercial sensitivity. Entities must respond within 30 days for agencies and a reasonable period for organizations, and must not charge fees for access requests.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures individuals have access to their personal information for cross-border transfers, with clear timelines and fee restrictions to prevent barriers to data access.

requirements.text: Entity must provide access to personal information upon request
Entity must respond within 30 days for agencies
Entity must not charge individuals for access requests

sections.source_quote: If an APP entity holds personal information about an individual, the entity must, on request by the individual, give the individual access to the information.

sections.heading: Correction
sections.title: Correction of personal information requests
sections.summary: An APP entity must correct inaccurate, outdated, incomplete, irrelevant, or misleading personal information when the entity determines it is inaccurate or when an individual requests correction. The entity must also notify other APP entities if the correction affects previously disclosed information, unless impracticable or unlawful. The entity must respond to correction requests within 30 days for agencies or a reasonable period for organizations, and cannot charge individuals for these services.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires entities to correct personal information upon request and notify third parties when corrections affect previously disclosed data, impacting cross-border data transfer where corrections are made to international entities.

requirements.text: If an APP entity holds personal information about an individual and the entity determines the information is inaccurate, outdated, incomplete, irrelevant or misleading, the entity must correct it.

If an APP entity corrects personal information previously disclosed to another APP entity and the individual requests notification of the correction, the entity must notify the other entity unless impracticable or unlawful.
If an APP entity refuses to correct personal information as requested, the entity must provide written notice of reasons for refusal, available complaint mechanisms, and any other prescribed matters.
If an APP entity refuses to correct personal information and the individual requests a statement of inaccuracy, the entity must associate the statement in a way that makes it apparent to users.
For correction requests, an agency must respond within 30 days after the request is made, while an organization must respond within a reasonable period.
An APP entity must not charge individuals for making correction requests, correcting information, or associating statements of inaccuracy.

sections.source_quote: 13.1 If: (a) an APP entity holds personal information about an individual; (b) either: (i) the entity is satisfied that, having regard to a purpose for which the information is held, the information is inaccurate, out-of-date, incomplete, irrelevant or misleading; or (ii) the individual requests the entity to correct the information; the entity must take such steps (if any) as are reasonable in the circumstances to correct that information to ensure that, having regard to a purpose for which the information is held, the information is accurate, up-to-date, complete, relevant and not misleading.

sections.heading: Correction (cont.)
sections.title: Effect of apology on privacy liability
sections.summary: This section clarifies that an apology made by a defendant in connection with an invasion of privacy does not constitute an admission of fault or liability. It also states that an apology is not relevant to determining fault or liability in privacy cases. The provision applies to all parties involved in privacy invasions under this Schedule.
sections.dtia_summary: Apologies do not automatically resolve privacy liability; courts must still assess fault and liability separately. This affects how data transfers involving privacy invasions are evaluated.

requirements.text: An apology made by or on behalf of the defendant in connection with the invasion of privacy does not constitute an express or implied admission of fault or liability
An apology made by or on behalf of the defendant in connection with the invasion of privacy is not relevant to the determination of fault or liability

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Schedule, an apology made by or on behalf of the defendant in connection with the invasion of privacy: (a) does not constitute an express or implied admission of fault or liability by the defendant in connection with the invasion of privacy; and (b) is not relevant to the determination of fault or liability in connection with the invasion of privacy.

sections.heading: Correction (cont.)
sections.title: Correction procedures for data transfers
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for correcting errors in data transfers across borders. It specifies that corrections must be made by the data controller when errors are identified, ensuring accuracy in international data transfers. The section applies to entities handling personal data that require cross-border transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires data controllers to correct errors in international data transfers, which impacts DTIA by mandating error correction mechanisms for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Corrections must be made by the data controller when errors are identified
